data.title: apply-changeset validates branch name against strict regex to prevent shell injection
data.claim: The apply-changeset workflow MUST capture `head.ref` into an environment variable and validate it against `^knowledge/batch-[0-9]{4}-[0-9]{2}-[0-9]{2}(-[0-9]+)?$` before any shell use — direct interpolation of `head.ref` into shell commands is a shell injection vector.
data.body:
## Background
CodeRabbit security review on ether-moon/skill-set#11 identified that the original apply-changeset workflow interpolated `github.event.pull_request.head.ref` directly into shell commands. A branch name containing shell metacharacters could execute arbitrary commands in the workflow runner.

## Details
The fix has two parts: (1) assign `head.ref` to an environment variable (`BRANCH: ${{ github.event.pull_request.head.ref }}`), and (2) validate it with a bash regex guard before use:
```bash
if [[ ! "$BRANCH" =~ ^knowledge/batch-[0-9]{4}-[0-9]{2}-[0-9]{2}(-[0-9]+)?$ ]]; then
  echo "::error::Unexpected branch format: ${BRANCH}"
  exit 1
fi
```
This pattern must be replicated in any workflow that uses `head.ref` or other user-controllable values in shell commands.

## Constraint Alignment
The regex must remain in sync with the branch naming convention produced by the batch-refine skill (`knowledge/batch-YYYY-MM-DD` or `knowledge/batch-YYYY-MM-DD-N`). If the naming convention changes, both the skill and this validation must be updated together.

## Rejected Alternatives
Using `startsWith()` alone (as in the `if:` condition) is insufficient for shell safety — it only gates job execution but does not sanitize the value for use inside `run:` steps.
data.considerations: This pattern applies to any GitHub Actions workflow that uses user-controlled values (branch names, PR titles, comment bodies) in `run:` steps. Always prefer env-var assignment + validation over direct interpolation.

data.title: curate-report gates /curate trigger on author_association, not bot-identity check
data.claim: The curate-report workflow MUST use `contains(fromJSON('["OWNER","MEMBER","COLLABORATOR"]'), github.event.comment.author_association)` to gate `/curate` triggers — a 'not a bot' check alone is insufficient and allows any authenticated GitHub user to trigger curation.
data.body:
## Background
The original curate-report trigger checked that the commenter was not a bot. CodeRabbit identified this as insufficient: any authenticated non-bot GitHub user could post `/curate` and trigger the workflow, potentially injecting curated entries into the vault.

## Details
The fix replaces the bot-exclusion check with an explicit author association allowlist using the GitHub Actions `fromJSON` array idiom:
```yaml
contains(fromJSON('["OWNER","MEMBER","COLLABORATOR"]'), github.event.comment.author_association)
```
This restricts triggering to repository owners, organization members, and explicitly added collaborators. CONTRIBUTOR (one-time committers) and NONE (public users) are excluded.

## Why fromJSON
`fromJSON(...)` is required because GitHub Actions `if:` expressions do not support array literals natively. The pattern `contains(fromJSON('["A","B"]'), value)` is the idiomatic membership check.

## Rejected Alternatives
Checking `github.event.comment.author_association != 'NONE'` was considered but rejected — it would still allow CONTRIBUTOR-level users who had one merged PR but no ongoing relationship with the project.
data.considerations: The three allowed associations (OWNER, MEMBER, COLLABORATOR) cover all intentional maintainers. If guest curators are needed, they must be added as explicit COLLABORATOR rather than relaxing the gate.

data.title: .mcp.json must be gitignored — it is generated at runtime and may contain API secrets
data.claim: `.mcp.json` MUST be listed in `.gitignore` in any project using Claude Code MCP tooling — the file is generated at runtime by Claude Code and may contain API keys, connection strings, or other secrets that must not be version-controlled.
data.body:
## Background
The init template previously did not exclude `.mcp.json` from version control. CodeRabbit flagged this during the skill-set#11 review: projects initializing the Knowledge Distillery plugin might accidentally commit `.mcp.json` if they ran `git add .` after an MCP configuration step.

## Details
`.mcp.json` is a local runtime configuration file created by Claude Code to store MCP server configurations, which can include API keys and authentication tokens. It is analogous to `.env` for environment secrets — it should never be committed. The init template now includes this entry in its generated `.gitignore`:
```
.mcp.json
```

## Rejected Alternatives
Relying on users to manually gitignore `.mcp.json` was rejected because the init workflow generates project scaffolding and should produce a safe configuration by default.
data.considerations: This applies to the init template and any project created by the init skill. Existing projects without this `.gitignore` entry should add it manually. Check `git status` for `.mcp.json` before committing.

data.title: knowledge-gate queries return summary index by default; full bodies require explicit get/get-many
data.claim: All knowledge-gate query commands (query-paths, query-domain, search, list) MUST return a lightweight summary index (id, type, title, claim) by default — full entry bodies are only available via `get <id>` or `get-many <id...>`.
data.body:
## Background
Analysis of the codenamev/claude_memory project identified progressive disclosure (cheap summary → targeted detail fetch) as the highest-value adoptable pattern. The previous knowledge-gate had inconsistent output policies: some commands returned summaries, others returned full bodies, with no explicit design intent.

## Details
After PR #28, the summary-first workflow is:
1. Run a query command (query-paths, query-domain, search, or list) → receive lightweight summary index
2. Review summaries to identify relevant entries by id
3. Run `get <id>` or `get-many <id1> <id2> ...` to fetch full bodies only for the relevant subset

This reduces per-query token cost while keeping the context-gate intact. The summary index fields are: `id`, `type`, `title`, `claim`.

## Rejected Alternatives
Returning full bodies by default was the previous inconsistent behavior. It was rejected because large vaults would flood agent context with irrelevant content on every query.

## Stop Conditions
If a query reliably returns ≤2 entries (e.g., `get` by exact ID), full-body output may be appropriate. A future `--full` flag for small-result cases is an open question.
data.considerations: AGENTS.md and the knowledge-gate SKILL.md document this workflow. The pre-prompt hook now emits summary-first guidance. Any skill or hook that queries knowledge-gate must follow the discovery-then-fetch flow.

data.title: Summary index MUST include the claim field — omitting it forces full-body fetches for all entries
data.claim: The summary index returned by knowledge-gate queries MUST include the `claim` field (minimum: id, type, title, claim) — without `claim`, an agent cannot judge entry relevance from the summary alone and is forced to fetch full bodies for every result, negating the purpose of the summary-first design.
data.body:
## Background
When designing the summary index field set, there was a question of whether `id + title` alone was sufficient for agents to decide which entries needed full bodies. The team identified that `title` alone is often ambiguous (e.g., 'Constraint on X behavior') and that the claim — which is the prescriptive statement of the rule — is what an agent actually needs to judge whether the entry is relevant to a given task.

## Details
Without `claim` in the summary, an agent seeing the title 'Tests must remain shell/jq-based' cannot determine whether the full body is needed for a JavaScript-related task — it must fetch the full body to be sure. With `claim` in the summary, the agent can read 'MUST remain shell/jq-based to align with current tooling; do not add a Python test dependency' and immediately determine relevance.

The minimum summary index fields are: `id` (for fetch), `type` (for filtering), `title` (for human readability), `claim` (for relevance judgment).

## Rejected Alternatives
Id-only and id+title outputs exist as lighter modes (`--ids-only` and implicit narrow output), but neither is the default because both require an extra round-trip to judge relevance.
data.considerations: If the claim field becomes excessively long in future entry types, a `short_claim` or `claim_preview` truncation may be needed. For now, the full claim is the right field.

data.title: get-many must fail fast when any requested ID is absent
data.claim: `knowledge-gate get-many` MUST exit with status 1 and emit `ERROR: entry IDs not found: <ids>` when any requested entry ID is not in the vault — silently returning a shorter array causes agents to proceed on incomplete information without detecting the gap.
data.body:
## Background
The initial implementation of `get-many` fetched all available entries for the requested IDs and returned whatever was found. CodeRabbit flagged that this silent drop of missing IDs was misleading: if an agent requests 3 IDs and receives 2 entries, it has no way to know whether the third was intentionally absent or a bug in the ID list.

## Details
The implementation uses a LEFT JOIN between the requested IDs and the vault entries, then checks for NULLs:
```bash
if any_null_found; then
  echo "ERROR: entry IDs not found: $missing_ids" >&2
  exit 1
fi
```
This makes get-many behave like a strict batch fetch: either all requested entries are returned, or the command fails with a clear error identifying which IDs were missing.

## Rejected Alternatives
Best-effort / partial return was rejected because it trades correctness for leniency. An agent that asks for 3 entries and receives 2 silently is likely to build on the assumption all 3 were present.
data.considerations: This fail-fast behavior means callers should only request IDs they expect to exist in the vault. If an entry may have been removed or renamed, use `search` or `list` first to confirm the ID before using `get-many`.

data.title: knowledge-gate query commands reject unknown flags with a usage error
data.claim: knowledge-gate query commands (query-paths, query-domain, search) MUST reject unrecognized flags with a usage message via a `-*)` catch-all case — previously, unknown flags like `--id-only` (a typo for `--ids-only`) were silently accepted as the positional argument, returning empty results with no indication of error.
data.body:
## Background
The original query parsers in the knowledge-gate script used option-parsing loops that handled known flags but had no catch-all for unknown flags. If a user typed `--id-only` instead of `--ids-only`, the misspelled flag was treated as the positional argument (domain/path/keyword), causing the query to return empty results silently.

## Details
Each query parser now includes a catch-all case:
```bash
-*)
  echo "Unknown flag: $1" >&2
  echo "Usage: knowledge-gate query-domain [--ids-only] <domain>" >&2
  exit 1
  ;;
```
This turns a silent empty-results failure into an explicit error that identifies the unrecognized flag and exits with non-zero status.

## Rejected Alternatives
Warning-but-continue was considered but rejected — if the flag was intended to change behavior (e.g., `--ids-only`), continuing without it silently produces wrong output.
data.considerations: The catch-all must be placed after all known flag cases in the option-parsing loop. This pattern should be applied to any new flags or commands added to knowledge-gate in the future.

data.title: --ids-only is opt-in ultra-light mode; summary index is the correct default
data.claim: `--ids-only` MUST remain an opt-in flag for bulk candidate pre-filtering, not the default output mode — summary index (id+type+title+claim) is the correct default because IDs alone require an additional full round-trip to judge relevance.
data.body:
## Background
When designing the two lightweight output modes — summary index and `--ids-only` — the team evaluated whether `--ids-only` should be the default to maximize token savings. It was explicitly rejected as default.

## Details
The two modes serve different use cases:
- **Summary index (default)**: Returns id + type + title + claim. Sufficient for most relevance judgments without a second fetch. Use for typical query → judge → selective fetch flows.
- **--ids-only (opt-in)**: Returns only IDs. Useful when the caller already knows what IDs it wants to pass to `get-many` for filtering large candidate sets, or when minimal output is critical.

Making `--ids-only` the default would force every agent query to require an additional `get-many` call just to see the claim, doubling the round-trips for the common case.

## Rejected Alternatives
`--ids-only` as default was considered and rejected. The token savings from returning only IDs do not justify the mandatory extra round-trip for claim lookup in the typical use case.
data.considerations: If vault entries grow very large and summary index tokens become a concern, consider claim truncation before reverting to id-only defaults. The claim field is load-bearing for single-pass relevance judgment.
